generatedBy: LLM-assisted (see prompts.md for exact prompts used)

testCases.title: API request with a valid auth token succeeds
preconditions: User has authenticated and obtained a valid access token
steps: Obtain a valid access token via login/auth endpoint
Send a GET request to a protected endpoint with the token in the Authorization header
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 200 OK and the requested resource data.

testCases.title: API request with a missing auth token is rejected
steps: Send a GET request to a protected endpoint without an Authorization header
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 401 Unauthorized and a clear error message; no protected data is returned.

testCases.title: API request with an expired token is rejected
preconditions: An expired access token is available (previously valid, now past expiry)
steps: Obtain an expired token (or wait for a short-lived token to expire)
Send a request to a protected endpoint using the expired token
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 401 Unauthorized; error body indicates the token has expired (e.g., 'token_expired').

testCases.title: API request with a malformed or tampered token is rejected
steps: Construct a malformed token (invalid JWT structure) or tamper with a valid token's payload/signature
Send a request to a protected endpoint using this token
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 401 Unauthorized; signature/format validation fails safely without exposing stack traces or internal errors.

testCases.title: Refresh token successfully issues a new valid access token
preconditions: User has a valid refresh token
steps: Send a refresh request with a valid refresh token
Verify the response contains a new access token
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 200 OK and issues a new valid access token (and optionally a new refresh token); old access token behavior follows the app's defined rotation policy.

testCases.title: Revoked/logged-out token is rejected on subsequent requests
preconditions: User has logged out or explicitly revoked their token
steps: Authenticate and obtain a valid token
Log out (or call a token-revocation endpoint)
Attempt to use the same token on a protected endpoint
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 401 Unauthorized; revoked token cannot be reused even though it hasn't naturally expired.

testCases.title: Token issued for one user cannot access another user's scoped resources
preconditions: Two distinct user accounts exist, each with their own resources
steps: Authenticate as User A and obtain a valid token
Attempt to access or modify a resource owned exclusively by User B using User A's token
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 403 Forbidden (or 404, per app convention); no cross-user data leakage or modification occurs (broken object-level authorization check).

testCases.title: Create (POST) a new resource with valid payload succeeds
preconditions: User is authenticated with permission to create the resource
steps: Send a POST request with a complete, valid payload
Inspect the response
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 201 Created; response body includes the newly created resource with a generated ID; resource is retrievable via a subsequent GET.

testCases.title: Read (GET) an existing resource by ID returns correct data
preconditions: A resource with a known ID exists
steps: Send a GET request using the ID of a known, existing resource
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 200 OK and a response body accurately reflecting the resource's current state.

testCases.title: Read (GET) a list endpoint supports pagination correctly
preconditions: More records exist than fit on a single page
steps: Send a GET request for a list endpoint with page and limit query parameters
Verify the returned page of results and metadata (total count, next/prev page)
testCases.expectedResult: API returns the correct subset of records for the requested page along with accurate pagination metadata; no duplicate or skipped records across pages.

testCases.title: Update (PUT/PATCH) an existing resource with valid data succeeds
preconditions: A resource with a known ID exists
steps: Send a PATCH request to update one or more fields of an existing resource
Send a GET request to verify persistence
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 200 OK and the updated resource; only the specified fields change (partial update via PATCH); GET confirms the change persisted.

testCases.title: Update a non-existent resource returns 404
steps: Send a PATCH/PUT request using an ID that does not exist
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 404 Not Found; no resource is created as a side effect.

testCases.title: Delete (DELETE) an existing resource succeeds and resource is no longer retrievable
preconditions: A resource with a known ID exists
steps: Send a DELETE request for an existing resource
Send a subsequent GET request for the same resource ID
testCases.expectedResult: DELETE responds with 200 OK or 204 No Content; the follow-up GET returns 404 Not Found, confirming removal.

testCases.title: Deleting an already-deleted resource is handled gracefully (idempotency)
preconditions: A resource has already been deleted
steps: Delete a resource
Send the same DELETE request again for the same resource ID
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 404 Not Found (or 204, per idempotency design) without throwing a server error (5xx).

testCases.title: Create resource with missing required fields is rejected
steps: Send a POST request omitting a required field (e.g., 'title')
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 400 Bad Request; response body clearly identifies the missing/invalid field(s); no partial resource is created.

testCases.title: Requesting a non-existent resource returns 404 with a structured error body
steps: Send a GET request for a resource ID that does not exist
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 404 Not Found; body follows a consistent error schema (e.g., { error, message, statusCode }) rather than an empty body or stack trace.

testCases.title: Sending malformed JSON in the request body returns 400
steps: Send a POST request with syntactically invalid JSON in the body
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 400 Bad Request and a parse-error message; server does not crash or return a 500.

testCases.title: Using an unsupported HTTP method on a valid endpoint returns 405
steps: Send a request using an HTTP method not supported by the endpoint (e.g., PATCH on a login endpoint that only supports POST)
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 405 Method Not Allowed and, ideally, an 'Allow' header listing supported methods.

testCases.title: Accessing a resource without sufficient permissions returns 403, not 500
preconditions: Authenticated user lacks permission for the target resource/action
steps: Authenticate as a user without admin privileges
Attempt an admin-only action (e.g., delete another user)
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 403 Forbidden and a clear message; server does not throw an unhandled exception (500).

testCases.title: Unexpected server-side failure returns a generic 500 without leaking internals
preconditions: A backend dependency (e.g., database) can be simulated as unavailable
steps: Simulate an internal failure (e.g., mock the database/service to throw an error)
Send a valid request that depends on the failing component
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 500 Internal Server Error and a generic, non-revealing error message; no stack trace, SQL, or internal file paths are exposed in the response.

testCases.title: Error responses follow a consistent schema across all endpoints
steps: Trigger error responses (4xx) across multiple different endpoints
Compare the structure/shape of each error response body
testCases.expectedResult: All error responses share a consistent JSON schema (e.g., consistent keys for error code, message, and details), simplifying client-side error handling.

testCases.title: Requests within the allowed rate limit succeed normally
preconditions: Rate limit is configured (e.g., 100 requests/minute per token)
steps: Send a number of requests below the configured rate limit threshold within the time window
testCases.expectedResult: All requests succeed with their normal expected status codes; rate-limit headers (if present) show remaining quota decreasing correctly.

testCases.title: Exceeding the rate limit returns 429 Too Many Requests
preconditions: Rate limit is configured (e.g., 100 requests/minute per token)
steps: Rapidly send requests exceeding the configured threshold within the time window
Observe the response once the limit is crossed
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 429 Too Many Requests once the threshold is exceeded; response includes a Retry-After header or equivalent indicating when to retry.

testCases.title: Rate limit resets correctly after the time window elapses
preconditions: Client has been rate-limited (429 received)
steps: Exceed the rate limit to trigger a 429 response
Wait for the configured rate-limit window to fully elapse
Send another request
testCases.expectedResult: Request succeeds normally after the window resets; quota counters reflect a fresh window.

testCases.title: Rate limiting is applied per client/token, not globally across all users
preconditions: Two distinct authenticated clients/tokens are available
steps: Exhaust Client A's rate limit by sending requests up to the threshold
Immediately send a request as Client B using a different token
testCases.expectedResult: Client B's request succeeds normally; Client A's rate limiting does not affect Client B (limits are scoped per token/user/IP as designed).

testCases.title: Successful response body conforms to the documented/expected JSON schema
preconditions: OpenAPI/JSON schema definition exists for the endpoint

testData.schema: TestCase schema (id: string, title: string, module: string, priority: enum, createdAt: date-time)

steps: Send a valid GET request to the endpoint
Validate the response body against the defined JSON schema (types, required fields, formats)
testCases.expectedResult: Response body passes schema validation with no missing required fields, incorrect types, or unexpected extra fields (per schema strictness policy).

testCases.title: Request payload with an incorrect data type for a field is rejected
steps: Send a POST request where a field's value has an incorrect data type (e.g., number instead of enum string for 'priority')
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 400 Bad Request; validation error clearly identifies the field and expected type.

testCases.title: Response field types remain consistent across repeated calls and different data states
preconditions: Multiple resource instances exist with varying field values (including nulls/empty states)
steps: Call the list endpoint multiple times across records with different states (e.g., a record with an empty description vs. one with a full description)
Validate that field types never shift (e.g., a field is never string in one record and null-as-number in another) against the schema
testCases.expectedResult: All records conform to the same schema and types consistently, including correct handling of optional/nullable fields.

testCases.title: Enum fields only accept defined values and reject arbitrary strings
preconditions: A field is constrained to a defined enum (e.g., priority: P1/P2/P3)
steps: Send a POST/PATCH request with an enum field set to a value outside the defined allowed set
testCases.expectedResult: API responds with 400 Bad Request, rejecting the invalid enum value; error message lists the allowed values.

testCases.title: API rejects unexpected/extra fields in the request payload per the app's strictness policy
preconditions: API is configured to either strictly reject or safely ignore unknown fields
steps: Send a POST request including a field not defined in the schema
Observe API behavior
testCases.expectedResult: API either rejects the request with 400 (strict mode) or safely ignores the unknown field without persisting or reflecting it (lenient mode) — per the documented contract; behavior is consistent, not undefined.
